name: Erbium
name_origin: Named after the village of Ytterby in Sweden, source of several rare-earth minerals.

occurrence.abundance_summary: Moderately abundant lanthanide (~3.5 ppm in crust); concentrated in xenotime, monazite, gadolinite, and ion-adsorption clays.

compounds.name: Erbium(III) oxide
compounds.summary: Pink solid used as a glass and ceramic colourant and as a precursor for erbium-doped optical materials.

compounds.name: Erbium(III) chloride
compounds.summary: Violet hygroscopic salt used as a precursor to erbium metal and erbium-doped laser host materials.

compounds.name: Erbium(III) fluoride
compounds.summary: Used in the manufacture of erbium-doped fluoride glass fibres for amplifiers.

compounds.name: Erbium-doped yttrium aluminium garnet
compounds.summary: Active medium for 2.94 micrometre lasers used in dermatology, dentistry, and ophthalmic surgery.

uses: Erbium-doped fibre amplifiers (EDFAs) for long-haul fibre-optic telecommunications at 1.55 micrometres
Er:YAG lasers (2.94 micrometres) for dermatological resurfacing, dentistry, and eye surgery
Pink colourant for glass, glazes, and cubic-zirconia gemstones
Neutron-absorbing additive in nuclear fuel and burnable poisons
Alloying additive that improves machinability of vanadium and other metals
reactivity_summary: Silvery-white ductile lanthanide that resists oxidation more than its lighter neighbours; tarnishes slowly in moist air. Reacts slowly with water and dissolves readily in dilute acids; the powdered metal can ignite in air.
isotopes_summary: Erbium has six naturally occurring stable isotopes (Er-162, 164, 166, 167, 168, 170). Er-166 is the most abundant (~33.5%). The longest-lived radioisotope is Er-169 (half-life 9.4 days).

citations.source: Royal Society of Chemistry - Periodic Table: Erbium
IUPAC Periodic Table of the Elements (2021)
CRC Handbook of Chemistry and Physics, 102nd Edition
